data.title: WDI: Collect High-Yield Income From This Discounted, Diversified Fixed Income Fund
data.description:
In my recent article covering 7 high-yield fixed income CEFs that are trading at a wider than average discount, one of those that I mentioned is Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI).

One of the criteria for evaluating whether a CEF is a potential buy candidate is the current discount (or premium) to NAV that the fund trades at based on the latest market price. One advantage to investing in CEFs is the ability to buy funds that are trading at a wider than average discount, because the fund managers often take steps to close that discount, or because the market adjusts the price closer to NAV after positive market sentiment drives the price higher. Either way, there is typically a price pattern that can be taken advantage of based on the trading history of a specific CEF.

Although WDI is less than five years old, it has followed a pattern similar to its peer funds like KKR Income Opportunities Fund (KIO), Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC), and PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) in the past three years delivering similar (and in fact, better) total returns than those multi-asset fixed income funds.

In this review, I want to take a more detailed look at WDI and suggest that income investors with a long-term horizon (ten to twenty years or more), might want to consider taking a position in this fund now while it trades at a much wider than average discount.

Before I get into the details on WDI, I want to reiterate my reasoning behind the recent market price action that has negatively impacted fixed income funds. Interest rates are one factor and we witnessed in 2022 how rapidly rising inflation caused interest rates to soar from near zero to over 4% in a matter of months. That negatively impacted WDI especially, which had just launched in June 2021. We can easily see on the 5-year price chart how WDI lost a lot of value in 2022 before recovering in 2024 as rates began to stabilize.

The fund’s holdings in high-yield bonds, bank loans, and CLOs make up the majority of the fund portfolio and all three of those types of fixed income securities delivered strong performance in Q4 2025 as explained in the performance review section of the fund commentary. Another factor contributing to positive Q4 performance was the securitized credit exposure, specifically the MBS securities in the fund portfolio.

  • The Fund's exposure to high-yield bonds, bank loans and CLOs was in aggregate the most significant contributor during the quarter given their high income generation.
  • The Fund's securitized credit exposure, mainly to non-agency residential and commercial mortgage-backed securities, was also a positive contributor.
  • Emerging markets exposure, particularly to USD-denominated sovereigns, contributed modestly to the Fund's performance.
  • The Fund's duration and curve positioning was a slight contributor as the UST yield curve twisted steeper, with shorter-term yields falling, while longer-term yields rose.

Although 2025 ended on a positive note, the start of 2026 has seen a lot of fear due at least in part to the private credit scare, driving down market prices of many of the fixed income funds that I have been following. WDI is no exception and saw its market price decline from what was a small premium in the summer of 2025 to near its widest discount in a year now at -5.5% (as of 4/10/26).

WDI Overview and Key Facts

WDI is a multisector bond fund with some interesting characteristics that set it apart from other similar funds. The fund description from the website spells out the details of the fund strategy and some key facts, including the termination provision. All twelve of the CEFs that were launched in 2021 had limited terms of 12 years before they either liquidate (at NAV) or solicit votes to convert to perpetual prior to the termination date.

Diverse opportunity set: Invests in a wide range of fixed income securities, seeking to go beyond traditional bond benchmarks to access a broad range of opportunities for income and capital appreciation. Income-focused: Potential access to fixed income sectors and private debt not typically available through traditional mutual funds. Flexible and dynamic: Rotates sectors and securities in response to market conditions, focusing on what we believe are undervalued securities with attractive fundamentals. Limited Term: The Fund intends to liquidate on or about June 24, 2033.

While the fund still has seven years before it faces the termination decision, that characteristic can help to drive a narrowing of the discount as the fund approaches the liquidation date. Therefore, it is something to keep in mind for long-term investors.

As of 2/28/26 according to the fund website, WDI had just over $1.1B in total assets employing about 33% leverage (in the form of loans and reverse repos) and portfolio holdings had an effective duration of just under 4 years, while the weighted average life of the holdings (days until maturity) was about 8.5 years.

The other interesting point is the flexible and dynamic nature of the portfolio holdings, which can be adjusted based on current market conditions. As an income-focused fund, there are opportunities available when market prices are driven lower due to market sentiment but fundamentals remain intact. I believe that is the case right now in mid-April 2026 as the recent impacts of the Iran war have pushed oil prices higher sending a ripple effect through global markets due to rising fears of rapidly increasing inflation, just like we witnessed in 2022.

According to the fund website, the largest concentration in the portfolio as of 3/31/26 was in High-Yield Credit at 45% followed by CLOs, CMBS, and bank loans. Non-agency MBS and a smattering of other fixed income instruments make up the remainder.

The fund sponsor, Western Asset, is one of the Franklin Templeton investment managers with over 50 years’ experience (originally founded in 1971) as an active fixed income manager. As mentioned in the fund description, the fund managers have access to fixed income securities and private debt not typically available to other mutual funds.

The top holdings shown on the fund website indicate a position size under 1% for all but the top 4, which together comprise just over 5% of the total portfolio value. The concentration risk is minimized with that level of diversification.

WDI Distribution History

Although it has a relatively short history of less than 5 years since going public, WDI has increased the monthly distribution several times and has paid out “special” distributions each year by making two distributions in the month of December.

In January of this year the payment date was changed to the end of the month so it appears on the chart that a month was skipped, however, that is not the case.

As of 4/13/26 the yield at market price was about 13% and 12.4% at NAV as shown on the fund website.

Dividend Reinvestment Plan

The fund does offer a DRIP (Dividend Reinvestment Plan) discount when shares are trading at a premium. The full text of the DRIP is spelled out in the Annual Report (see page 105) and I have included a summary of the relevant paragraphs for those interested in taking advantage of reinvested distributions, which can help to compound your future income stream even faster.

When the fund trades at a premium to NAV:

Fund will issue new Common Shares at a price equal to the greater of (A) the net asset value per share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the payment date or (B) 95% of the market price per share of the Common Shares on the payment date.

When the fund trades at a discount to NAV:

Fund will buy Common Shares in the open market, on the NYSE or elsewhere, for your account as soon as practicable commencing on the trading day following the payment date and terminating no later than the earlier of (A) 30 days after the dividend or distribution payment date, or (B) the payment date for the next succeeding dividend or distribution to be made to the shareholders.

Since its inception, the fund has rarely traded at a premium so the DRIP discount is something to keep on your radar if you do want to take advantage of it when the price returns to premium territory at some point in the future.

WDI Performance

Because of the brief relative history of the fund, a chart of calendar year returns is probably the best way to review and evaluate past performance. From the fund website, the fund underperformed in 2022 like most other fixed income and equity funds that year, but delivered strong performance in 2023 followed by very good performance in 2024 and 2025. Performance slowed during the first quarter of 2026 as the market price declined even further than the NAV in the first two months of this year.

Risks to WDI Buy Thesis

The biggest risk is that the economy suddenly lurches into a deep global recession due to the impact of much higher oil prices as it begins to trickle down to negatively affect everything from the cost of airline fuel to farm fertilizer. This is a very real possibility that the market seems to be overlooking for now, but could become reality later this year if things continue as they have been for the past few weeks with no end in sight yet for the Iran conflict.

Other risks include a potential decision by the Fed to raise interest rates due to suddenly higher inflation in the next few months, suppressing growth and causing stock market declines to spillover into fixed income sending prices lower.

One way to offset those risks is by acquiring shares of the fund when the discount is substantially lower than average. The long-term track record of peer funds like PDI, GOF, KIO, and ARDC support the thesis of buying when the discount is low (or the premium is lower than average). Generally speaking, each of those funds have recovered from past market corrections within a matter of months and have followed very similar trading patterns for the past five years.

Summary: Buy WDI While Discount is Wide and Collect 13% Yield

Although the fund does not have a long track record to analyze it has many other things going for it. The fund is managed by an experienced management team with more than 50 years in the fixed income securities management business. The portfolio mix is well diversified and optimizes current market conditions with its mix of mostly high-yield corporate bonds along with senior loans and CLOs. The income from the portfolio holdings easily supports the high-yield distribution and typically pays out an additional or special distribution each year to meet the requirements of an RIC (Regulated Investment Company).

An RIC must earn at least 90% of its income from capital gains, interest, or dividends from investments. Further, an RIC must distribute a minimum of 90% of its net investment income in the form of interest, dividends, or capital gains to its shareholders.

According to the fund’s Annual Report, offering a steady, reliable monthly distribution is the goal.

The Fund has a practice of seeking to maintain a relatively stable level of distributions to shareholders. This practice has no impact on the Fund’s investment strategy and may reduce the Fund’s NAV. The Fund’s manager believes the practice helps maintain the Fund’s competitiveness and may benefit the Fund’s market price and premium/discount to the Fund’s NAV.

For a retiree seeking reliable monthly income now yielding 13%, I suggest taking a look at WDI.

data.title: Bank of America: A Higher-For-Longer Rate Play
data.description:
Higher energy prices and a potentially changing tune of the Federal Reserve with respect to rate cuts could be game-changing events this year for Bank of America Corporation (BAC). The bank just reported better-than-expected results for its first fiscal quarter, mainly due to strong activity in its consumer-oriented business. Most importantly, the bank recorded significant gains in its net interest income -- which rose 17% year-over-year amid a higher-for-longer interest rate environment -- and managed to expand its loan portfolio.

While the consensus has been until recently that the Federal Reserve will push for a federal fund rate cuts in 2026, an increase in inflation driven by the February start of a new war in the Middle East, has the potential to fundamentally strengthen the earnings picture of Bank of America. With the Fed likely going to delay rate cuts until inflation is back under control, the Wall Street bank faces an attractive interest rate landscape that could spur further NII gains in the quarters ahead.

Previous rating

I rated Bank of America a Buy despite growing rate cut expectations at the beginning of the year -- Buy The Pullback On NII Strength -- because the Wall Street bank exhibited significant net interest income strength. In the first-quarter, Bank of America generated considerable net interest income and shifting interest rate expectations have the potential to boost the bank's earnings further in the quarters ahead. Since the consumer banking business is in excellent shape and the loan portfolio is growing, I maintain a Buy rating following the Bank of America's Q1 '26 earnings scorecard.

Bank of America crushes Q4 ’25 expectations

Bank of America published higher-than-expected earnings and revenue results for Q1 ’26, supported by strong results in the core lending business as well as in consumer banking: the bank published $1.11 per-share in GAAP earnings vs. a consensus estimate of $1.02 per-share. Bank of America also sailed past top line estimates by reporting $30.3B in total revenue for the first-quarter, beating the estimate by ~$350M.

Bank of America is benefiting from a resilient and growing U.S. economy that fundamentally supports its core lending operations: the bank published $30.3B in total revenues for the first fiscal quarter, showing 7% year-over-year growth. Further, the Wall Street bank posted net income of $8.6B, highlighting a year-over-year increase of 21%, with BofA seeing specific growth tailwinds from its consumer-facing banking business in the U.S.

Bank of America’s financial results were mainly driven by three factors: 1) Strong growth in the consumer segment, 2) Higher net interest income (the most significant component), and 3) An expansion in its loan portfolio.

As to the first point, Bank of America's consumer operations -- mainly personal loans and other banking services -- contributed $3.1B in profits to the bank's total net earnings in the first fiscal quarter (+21% Y/Y). While not the fastest growth rate for BofA (Global Wealth and Investment Management saw a 32% Y/Y profit jump), the bank's consumer business is by far the largest.

Net interest income and resurgence of inflation

The March CPI report was not a good one for consumers, but it might turn out to be a good one for Wall Street banks that rely on high interest rates for their variable rate loan portfolios.

In March, inflation reaccelerated to 3.3% -- the highest level since March 2024 -- which was driven mainly by an increase in energy prices related to the war on Iran. Petroleum prices surged to more than $100 per barrel immediately after the outbreak of the war on February 28, 2026 and since energy prices are a core component of the inflation index, higher inflation figures strongly tilt the odds in favor of delayed rate cuts for the financial sector. This, of course, represents a major catalyst for the bank's net interest income, a key component for Bank of America's net earnings.

Bank of America posted $15.7B in net interest income in the fourth-quarter (+$1.3B, +9% Y/Y), highlighting a strong lending environment for banks with large loan portfolios. The bank benefited from both higher-for-longer interest rates as well as a larger loan portfolio which was driven especially by gains made in the credit market. Positive deposit and loan trends as well as fixed-rate asset repricing drove gains for Bank of America in this regard.

In the first quarter, Bank of America grew its average loans to $1.19T, showing a massive 9% year-over-year growth rate. This growth stemmed mostly from high demand for loans in the Global Markets and Global Wealth and Investment Management businesses. Most importantly, with the market suffering the above-mentioned increase in inflation, the Federal Reserve is now much less likely to lower federal fund rates in the short term. Since Bank of America estimates that a 100 (parallel) basis point shift to the downside will impact the bank's net interest income negatively by $2.0B over a twelve-months period, delayed rate cuts immediately benefit the bank's bottom line in the next several quarters.

Bank of America’s valuation

Bank of America has seen a significant valuation rebound in March, in-line with the broader market, but shares still have upside potential in my opinion, especially in the context of higher energy prices reaccelerating inflation. Shares of Bank America are currently valued at a price-to-book ratio of 1.38X compared to 1.53X for Wells Fargo (WFC). Both banks are set to be beneficiaries of delayed rate cuts as they own a considerable amount of variable rate-paying loans on their balance sheets.

In my last review of Bank of America I calculated a fair value of $64 per-share for the Wall Street bank -- based off of historical valuation ratios -- which calculates to ~20% upside potential. Most importantly, with the market now expecting a higher-for-longer rate environment, Bank of America actually has a catalyst for NII growth that previously did not exist. Therefore, I see my $64 price target for BofA more as a low-end fair value estimate.

Risks with Bank of America

The biggest risk for Bank of America right now would be a decline in petroleum prices which would likely precipitate lower core inflation. This in turn could result in an accelerating timeline of federal fund rate cuts which would negatively impact Bank of America’s earnings outlook in the short and medium term. What would therefore get me to change my mind about Bank of America is if the lender were to see a decline in its net interest income (lower NII margins) as well as slower loan growth.

Final thoughts

Bank of America submitted a very decent earnings sheet for the first fiscal quarter on Wednesday that beat analyst expectations, and that showed continued gains on the most important metric for banks: its net interest income. In Q1 ’26, Bank of America report $8.6B in net interest income showing a 21% year-over-year increase, due to a strong interest and loan backdrop.

I like that Bank of America is expanding its loan portfolio and posting strong consumer banking results, supported by robust economic performance in the U.S. With shares trading about 17% below my (unchanged) fair value target, I believe the risk profile here is skewed to the upside, mainly because the Federal Reserve has a strong incentive to postpone NII-eroding interest rate cuts.

data.title: Davenport Core Leaders Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Q1 2026 Market Review

The Davenport Core Leaders Fund (DAVPX) declined 6.18% for the quarter, slightly lagging the S&P 500® Index, which fell 4.33%. Market conditions during the quarter were unsettled, as early period rotation away from technology and software stocks gave way to a sharp rally in the energy sector amid intensifying Middle East tensions. Sector allocation was a key driver of relative performance, with underweight positioning in Energy and Consumer Staples weighing most heavily as leadership shifted. While stock-specific underperformance impacted results, these headwinds were partially offset by favorable sector positioning in Industrials and Materials, as well as an underweight exposure to Technology and Communication Services. Despite the range of challenges encountered during the quarter, our strategy remains consistent and focused on owning high quality, competitively advantaged growth companies that we believe are well positioned to deliver attractive long term results.

Fund Update

Contributors: The Fund benefited from exposure to energy and energy related companies during the first quarter as the conflict with Iran contributed to a sharp rise in oil prices. Notable positive impacts from companies with energy-aligned businesses were seen in both EOG Resources, Inc. (EOG) and Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR).

Despite broad weakness among technology stocks, individual holdings such as Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI) and Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) contributed meaningfully to quarterly performance, reflecting solid contribution within a sector where the Fund remained significantly underweight.

Detractors: During the first quarter, ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), Accenture PLC (ACN), and Microsoft Corp (MSFT) were among the largest detractors within the Fund, as renewed pressure across the Technology sector—particularly concerns around artificial intelligence driven disruption—weighed on performance. In addition, weakness among several Health Care holdings impacted results, with both Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG) and UnitedHealth Group, Inc. (UNH) declining during the period.

**Foreign Holding. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

Fund Activity

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG). Constellation Energy is a leading U.S. power generator whose growth potential is anchored in reliable baseload generation and demand for clean energy. The company operates the largest nuclear fleet in the United States, with approximately 22 gigawatts of capacity, generating more than 180 million megawatt hours annually. This scale provides Constellation with meaningful competitive advantages, including high barriers to entry, industry-leading capacity factors, and growing opportunities to secure long-term, premium priced contracts as data center demand and electrification trends intensify. Beyond nuclear, Constellation's natural gas, renewables, and storage assets enhance earnings resilience, while its investment-grade balance sheet, expanding free cash flow, and disciplined capital allocation framework support long-term growth. Collectively, these attributes position Constellation as a beneficiary of structural growth in U.S. electricity demand and support our decision to initiate a new position in the stock.

We also engaged in a range of investment weighting changes as we adjusted position sizing to reflect emerging valuation opportunities and concerns during a volatile quarter. We added to positions in several technology companies including Accenture PLC (ACN), Apple, Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corp (MSFT), and ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW). We also trimmed investments in some technology holdings including Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META).

Conclusion

The first quarter was marked by heightened volatility and rapid shifts in market leadership, creating a particularly challenging investment backdrop. Despite these challenges, we remain focused on the long term – aligning ourselves with high quality businesses that have durable competitive advantages, strong returns on capital and management teams committed to disciplined, long term capital allocation. Although this approach may lag during periods of abrupt leadership rotation, we believe it positions the Fund to compound intrinsic value more reliably over full market cycles. Looking ahead, we remain confident that staying grounded in business fundamentals—rather than attempting to time short term market dynamics—provides the most effective path to long term risk adjusted returns across a wide range of economic and market conditions.

Sincerely,

The index includes the same constituents as the capitalization weighted S&P 500, but each company in the S&P 500 EWI is allocated a fixed weight – or 0.2% of the index total at each quarterly rebalance. An investor cannot invest in these indices and their returns are not indicative of the performance of any specific investment.

data.title: Microsoft: Mispriced And Unfairly Tarnished With Overblown Capital Allocation Fears (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Oddball of The Mag 7 Cohort

Notwithstanding the recent bouts of volatility, the past year has still turned out to be a fairly lucrative period for US large caps, with the chief equity benchmark generating returns of close to 30%. One of the notable pockets that has supported the relatively strong performance of the S&P500 is the Mag 7 club, where 6 out of its 7 members have all generated positive returns ranging from around 20% to 98%! Microsoft (MSFT), on which I’ve maintained a hold-rated stance for close to a year now, is the only Mag 7 member that has struggled to get going, with a minor contraction of less than 1%. Now, after an underwhelming 12 months, is there a potential catalyst on the horizon which could help MSFT make up for lost ground?

Well, it’s worth noting that Microsoft has just entered its seasonally strongest quarter (the volume of multi-year contracts tends to spike in the June quarter, which is also Q4 for Microsoft). Crucially, in less than 2 weeks (the 29th of April to be specific), Microsoft will also release its Q3 results and guidance, which could prove to be decisive one way or the other.

A Topline Beat Of >2% And A Bottomline Beat of >7% In Q3 Could Be Appreciated By The Markets

The market’s initial focus of Microsoft’s Q3 earnings release will go towards ascertaining how well or poorly the company has fared against consensus expectations on the revenue and the EPS. Based on recent history (the last 3 years), I don’t see why investors shouldn’t be emboldened to brace for a beat, as the company has always managed to trump expectations on both fronts. The question then boils down to what degree of beat would be seen as noteworthy? Well, anything less than a 2% topline beat and a 7% bottom-line beat may be shrugged off, as that would be in keeping with what’s been seen over the past 12 quarters.

Investors may also want to consider that consensus expectations heading into Q3 are slightly on the higher side. Back in late-January, the mid-point of management’s revenue guidance pointed to a Q3 revenue figure of $81.2B, but consensus is now at $81.39B. Also, close to 80% of the Q3 EPS revisions over the past 3 months have been to the upside, prompting a figure of $4.07.

Strong Demand Backlog and Cash-Generating Prowess of MSFT’s Asset Base Help Allay CAPEX Spurt

Investors who’ve taken a cursory glance at MSFT’s most recent quarterly operating cash flow shouldn’t make the mistake of extrapolating that subdued $36B run rate for the whole year, as the December quarter has traditionally been the weakest from a cash inflow perspective (higher bonus payments, and inventory & working capital build up for the holiday season tend to leave an inimical mark on cash generation). Encouragingly, in the March quarter, cash generation would likely have stepped up in a big way (for context, operating cash flow has typically grown by over 60% between Q2 and Q3), and should help ameliorate some of the free cash flow [FCF] concerns that have emerged off late (FCF was down by -9% YoY and -77% QoQ in December).

Considerable investor scrutiny will also go towards MSFT’s CAPEX commitments as it has been trending higher for four straight quarters and is currently growing at a stunning annual pace of 66% YoY.

Such a ferocious pace of CAPEX growth also means that the annualized CAPEX bill is now being covered at less than 2x by MSFT’s annualized operating cash flow (something that has not been seen over the past decade).

I can appreciate some investors having concerns over these developments, but I don’t believe the step up in investments will turn out to be a white elephant of sorts. It wouldn’t be fair to postulate that MSFT is throwing money down the drain, because the backlog they’ve accumulated over time also suggests that the demand is just snowballing.

Take for instance, MSFT’s backlog proxy- commercial RPOs [Remaining Performance Obligations], which is a precursor of future revenue. Firstly, the current commercial RPO of $625B has a weighted average duration of two-and-a-half years, which offers a solid foundation of future revenue for the foreseeable future. And it’s not as if the RPO is just about eking out growth of single-digit percentages; rather, we’ve seen double-digit percentage growth for multiple quarters now, and in the most recent quarter, growth in the RPO more than doubled annually (while also growing at a pace of +50% sequentially) reflecting the growing momentum! One also has to consider that a good chunk of these bookings may potentially get repriced at higher prices given the ravenous appetite for token usage.

Another reason why investors shouldn’t get bogged down by MSFT’s increased spending appetite is because under the stewardship of Satya Nadella (who was appointed in 2014), the cash flow generating capabilities of MSFT’s asset base has only improved over time and is currently at record highs of 26.6% (this is a superior metric to Return on assets or ROA which can be unfairly skewed by accounting effects). If this metric was flatlining or declining, one could afford to have concerns over MSFT’s asset building initiatives, but that isn't the case.

MSFT’s GARP Credentials Are Now Shining Bright

Value-conscious investors may be interested to note that unlike the scenario during our previous coverage of MSFT, its value quotient now is a lot more attractive. To elaborate, over the past 5 years, MSFT’s forward earnings multiple has averaged over 30x (and back in April 2025, it was roughly on par). Now, MSFT is priced at only around 23x forward earnings, which works out to a nice 25% discount.

Now, I recognize that some investors may initially be inferring that MSFT is perhaps set to deliver a lower cadence of earnings growth, and that’s why we’ve seen a contraction in the valuation multiple. However, the reality is quite the opposite.

Over the last 6 months, consensus forward earnings have been revised upwards by close to 8%, so much so that you’re getting earnings growth that is on par with the earnings valuation multiple (~23% annual earnings growth against a 23x valuation multiple). Put another way, this implies a very attractive forward PEG [Price to earnings growth] ratio of roughly 1x (whereas traditionally on average, the forward PEG ratio has been priced at a +60% premium) which should attract investors who gravitate to GARP-style [Growth-at-a-reasonable-price] stocks.

Technicals- Risk-Reward Balance Is More Favorable, With Potentially Higher Interest From Mean-Reversion Chasing Investors

Using the Wycoff Method as a reference point on Microsoft’s weekly chart, one can see that the stock, which peaked at the $555 levels in July 2025 (overbought conditions were also validated by the RSI indicator at the lower end of the chart), then went through a bout of distribution through October 2025, before entering the more troubling phase of markdowns (lower-lows and lower-highs) which then went on for the next 5 months.

Interestingly, last month. we saw MSFT retest the pivot zone of $340-$370 (the red-highlighted zone which had previously served as resistance or support), and quickly bounce off from there, suggesting that we may be close to seeing some form of a bottom in the selling pressure. Even otherwise, investors may also want to consider that a few weeks back the RSI indicator had already pointed to oversold conditions. Given the favorable valuation quotient on offer, I won’t be surprised to see MSFT continuing to press the pedal on its share repurchase efforts of which 80% of its authorized $60B share buyback plan was still pending as of 31st December 2025.

What could potentially also drive an uptick in bullish interest in MSFT now is that amongst an equally weighted portfolio of tech stocks that make-up the S&P500, MSFT looks very oversold. We say this because traditionally, the relative strength [RS] ratio of MSFT against this cohort has averaged less than 11x. However, now the current RS, which is at 5-year lows, is trading at a wide 30% discount to its long-term mean. This could potentially stimulate higher interest from mean-reversion chasing investors who move funds around the technology stack of the S&P500.

A Few Concerns

While MSFT may appeal to value-conscious investors and those who like themes of mean-reversion, I don’t yet expect it to come under the radar of momentum players, as the stock is still trading below its key daily moving averages of 50 and 200 (a break above the latter would perhaps be needed for MSFT to see sustained bullish interest).

Then, as I laid out in a previous section of the article, I am not perturbed by MSFT’s step up in capital spending, but it would be remiss of me to not highlight that this could potentially bring some added margin pressure in the short-term. Already quarterly gross margins, which peaked in the second half of 2023 have been on a downward trajectory but given that two-thirds of MSFT’s CAPEX spend consists of investment in short-lived assets, depreciation costs will likely be a lot higher, thus putting further pressure on margins.

Having said that, I believe MSFT could ameliorate pressures here and also step up efficiency gains at scale, as they seek to deploy more of their in in-house MAIA 200 AI Accelerator in their data centers for inferencing purposes. Studies have shown that the MAIA 200 could potentially deliver 30% better performance per dollar than the existing generation of hardware that is present in most of their data centers.

Closing Thoughts

MSFT has turned out to be an underwhelming Mag 7 pick over the past year, but I am now prepared to go against the herd and overturn my hold rating on the stock to a buy, ahead of the company’s Q3 results, which is due in less than two weeks.

After a challenging cash flow quarter in December, I expect operating cash flow to meaningfully step up in the March quarter, and while higher CAPEX commitments may put off a fair few investors, I feel the MSFT management has previously already demonstrated a long-standing track record of extracting more operating cash from their growing asset base. The spectacular growth (both sequentially and annually) of MSFT’s sturdy backlog (due to be converted over a two-and-a-half year time frame) also suggests that MSFT is not throwing money down the drain. Adding to all this, I am also enthused by MSFT’s discounted valuation backdrop, which also suggests that the stock has been mispriced for the degree of forward growth it is offering.

data.title: Atlas Copco: A World-Class Business Trading At A Premium
data.description:
It has been almost three years since I published my first and only article about Atlas Copco (ATLCY) – the Swedish multinational industrial company. The company and stock are also belonging in the category of high-quality business, but trading for high valuation multiples. In the conclusion of my article, I wrote:

Atlas Copco is not only the largest company in Sweden by market capitalization, but it is also a high-quality business with a wide economic moat and a company that outperformed over several decades. However, I don't want to buy an industrial company right before a potential recession and Atlas Copco is also trading for a premium - increasing the risk of a steep sell-off in the coming quarters even more.

For those already invested in Atlas Copco, the stock remains a "Hold" and I don't think shorting a high-quality business is a good idea, but I also don't think buying Atlas Copco right now is a smart move as we might be getting better prices in the coming quarters and years. During the recession in the early 1990s or the Great Financial Crisis, the stock declined more than 60% and a similar scenario is possible this time.

Since my last article was published, the stock increased about 38% in value (and 44.5% when including dividends). Without much doubt and discussion, this is a solid performance over less than three years. Nevertheless, it underperformed the S&P 500, which gained about 55% in value in the same timeframe.

In the following article, I will argue once again why Atlas Copco is still a great company with a wide economic moat but still not a good investment. We will look at the growth potential in the years to come as well as the valuation multiples, which are still not adding up resulting in an overvalued stock.

Last Results

As always, we start by looking at the last results. On January 27, 2026, the company reported fourth quarter and full-year results for fiscal 2025. In the full year, revenue declined from SEK 176,771 million in fiscal 2024 to SEK 168,343 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 4.8% year-over-year top-line decline. Operating income also declined 10.6% year-over-year from SEK 38,116 million in the previous year to SEK 34,114 million in fiscal 2025. Finally, diluted earnings per share declined from SEK 6.10 in fiscal 2024 to SEK 5.42 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 11.1% year-over-year decline for the bottom line.

When looking at the fourth quarter results, the picture is similar (and maybe slightly worse). Revenue declined from SEK 45,988 million in Q4/24 to SEK 42,782 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 7.0% year-over-year decline. Operating income declined 15.5% from SEK 10,018 million in the same quarter last year to SEK 8,470 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted earnings per share declined 15.0% year-over-year from SEK 6.10 in the same quarter last year to SEK 1.36 in this quarter.

However, when discussing the results, we can’t stop at this point. We must look at the numbers in more detail and understand why Atlas Copco had to report declining revenue in fiscal 2025. Especially currency effects had a huge negative impact on results in fiscal 2025 (6% decline due to currency effects) and in the fourth quarter (11% decline due to currency effects). In fiscal 2025, revenue declined only 1% on an organic basis and in the fourth quarter it was flat on an organic basis.

Growth Potential

As we saw above, Atlas Copco declined primarily due to currency effects in the last few quarters. But even when accounting for currency effects, Atlas Copco still was not able to grow. For revenue growth in the coming quarters, we can look at the orders the company is receiving right now. And in the last three months, Atlas Copco received 8% more orders than in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2024.

Growth stemmed especially from North America (8% growth), Europe (10% growth) and Asia (13% growth) and we can see this as a positive sign for high growth rates in fiscal 2026 again. Nevertheless, the volume of orders received in fiscal 2025 is still below actual revenue in the same year – indicating that we should still be cautious. Management is also issuing a near-term outlook, however it is only one brief sentence:

Atlas Copco Group expects that the customer activity will remain at the current level.

Aside from a near-term outlook, Atlas Copco also has mid-to-long term targets and these remain stable. Management is expecting revenue to grow 8% on average per year over a business cycle. The company also has the ambition to grow faster than the most important competitors and growth should primarily be organic.

And aside from growing revenue, the company can also try to improve its margins. In the last 10 years, margins were pretty stable and therefore we should not expect the margins to suddenly improve. Additionally, Atlas Copco is not really using share buybacks as a capital allocation tool. The company is repurchasing shares from time to time but also issuing new shares (or using shares as incentives) and therefore the number of diluted shares outstanding was rather stable in the last few years. Overall, we should not expect higher growth rates than 8% or 9% for the bottom line.

While management is quite optimistic, analysts are much more pessimistic at this point. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2034, analysts are expecting revenue to grow only at a CAGR of 3.81%. It is certainly interesting that analysts are expecting the company to grow its revenue only in the low-to-mid single digits but the stock is trading for valuation multiples above 30 (we will get to this) – numbers that are not really adding up.

We can probably make the argument that analysts are too pessimistic, and management might be a little bit too optimistic although the company was able to grow in the high single digits in the last ten years (at least currency-adjusted).

The company certainly has the potential to grow in the years to come – for example by adding new growth avenues through new technologies. Liquid ring pumps (growing with a CAGR of 45% in the last few years) and industrial pumps (growing with a CAGR of 164% since 2020) are two examples.

Acquisitions

I mentioned above that Atlas Copco is trying to mostly grow organically, but acquisitions are also an important part of the company’s growth strategy. The company is usually not making huge acquisitions, but many small acquisitions. In fiscal 2025, 29 acquisitions were closed – the biggest being National Tank & Equipment, which is generating SEK 2,100 million in revenue and has 349 employees. Most of the companies Atlas Copco acquired have less than 50 employees and usually generate only a few hundred million Swedish Kroner. Over the last ten years, Atlas Copco made 160 acquisitions and about SEK 23 billion in revenue is stemming from these acquisitions.

I have mentioned several times in the past that I like the strategy of making rather small acquisitions instead of a few mega mergers. Small acquisitions can also fail and not really generate additional value for investors (and the business). However, there is a huge difference whether an acquisition of a company with only 50 employees fails or if an acquisition for several billions is failing.

One risk for companies making a lot of acquisitions is a balance sheet that must be described as rather risky. And Atlas Copco had SEK 6,471 million in short-term borrowings on December 31, 2025, as well as SEK 28,428 million in long-term borrowing. When comparing the total debt to the shareholders’ equity of SEK 110,383 million, we get a D/E ratio of 0.32.

Not only the D/E ratio seems no reason to worry, Atlas Copco also has SEK 15,523 million in cash and cash equivalents, which is enough to repay almost 45% of the outstanding debt. And when subtracting cash and cash equivalents from total debt levels, it would take about two years of operating income or free cash flow to repay the outstanding debt. These are certainly not metrics to be concern about at this point.

Wide Economic Moat

Atlas Copco is clearly struggling right now and has trouble growing at a solid pace. But we can assume this might be only temporary – especially as the Swedish company has most likely a wide economic moat around its business. Such an economic moat will probably enable the company to outpace its peers and also protect the business from losing market share.

When discussing whether a company has a wide economic moat around its business, we can look at several metric, starting with the outperformance of Atlas Copco versus the S&P 500 during the last 20 years. While the S&P 500 increased about 480% in value (which is a great performance), Atlas Copco increased 962% during the same timeframe (not including dividends).

Another important metric we can look are the margins over the last ten years – and we are especially asking the question whether the margins are stable. In the best case, margins are hardly fluctuating at all or slightly improving year over year. This is indicating pricing power for the business. And when looking at the margins Atlas Copco reported in the last ten years, we see an almost perfect example of stability and consistency.

Finally, we are looking at return on invested capital – another metric indicating a wide economic moat around a business. When a company is able to report an RoIC above 10% year after year, it is a good sign for an economic moat. In the last ten years, the company reported a return on invested capital of 33.32, but this is including 2018 (a positive outlier). In the last five years, however, return on invested capital was still 21.59% on average – a very high RoIC indicating a wide economic moat.

All this leads to high growth rates over the long run and stable margins and stable ROCE (return on capital employed). While revenue growth will certainly fluctuate from year to year, the company has reported currency-adjusted growth rates of 9% annually since 2015 and very stable margins.

Dividend

Atlas Copco also proposed an ordinary dividend of SEK 3.00 for fiscal 2025 – in line with the previous dividend. However, management also proposed an extra dividend of SEK 2.00, resulting in a total dividend of SEK 5.00 for investors. This is resulting in a dividend yield of 2.7% for the A share and a dividend yield of 3.1% for the B share (which is trading for a 10% lower stock price at the time of writing).

The total dividend is also resulting in a payout ratio of 92% for the company and this is a payout ratio, which should usually make us cautious. However, due to the ordinary dividend being only SEK 3.00, management is always able to pay a much lower dividend next year as the extra dividend is clearly showing the one-time nature of the additional dividend payments. And, in my opinion, the stable ordinary dividend is indicating that management is rather cautious right now due to declining earnings per share in fiscal 2025.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

We see Atlas Copco struggling a little bit right now and growth rates are clearly below the long-term average with analysts being rather optimistic. And these rather slow growth rates are combined with still high valuation multiples. At the time of writing, the stock is trading for 35.5 times earnings per share and free cash flow. The stock is clearly trading above the 10-year average of P/E ratio (which was 28.47) and above the 10-year average of P/FCF ratio (which was 28.67).

When looking at these valuation multiples, we certainly can’t call Atlas Copco a bargain at this point. Instead, the stock seems rather expensive at this point.

However, simple valuation multiples can sometimes be misleading and of course, they don’t reflect the growth potential of a business and therefore a discount cash flow calculation is probably the better choice to determine an intrinsic value. For such a calculation we must make several assumptions and, as always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10% and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (4,873 million in Q4/25).

Additionally, we are calculating with the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was SEK 28,282 million. Let’s be optimistic and assume Atlas Copco can grow 9% annually on average, followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of SEK 134.89.

Now we can make the argument, that the free cash flow of the last four quarters is not representative for the business and instead we are using the free cash flow of fiscal 2024 (which was SEK 32,651 million). In this case, we get an intrinsic value of SEK 155.73.

However, the B stock is still trading for SEK 161 making the stock overvalued in both calculations and, in my opinion, Atlas Copco is still not a good investment. We are already calculating with optimistic assumptions here and can also raise the question if the company can actually grow 9% annually in the years to come.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Atlas Copco remains expensive and is still not a good investment. The company remains a good business despite growth rates slowing down a bit right now. But the valuation multiples are still high, and the stock seems to be above its intrinsic value – making it only a mediocre investment. For Atlas Copco becoming a good investment, we need a solid setback at this point and the stock should decline at least 20% or 30% before it becomes attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SOLV Energy: Valuation Too Low Vs. Peers
data.description:
Summary

I am positive about SOLV Energy (MWH). My thesis is that the market may still be viewing MWH like a normal EPC contractor, when it really isn’t. Its installed base creates a stickier O&M revenue stream, and the current backlog gives strong near-term earnings visibility. As projects get larger, I think scale should also help MWH win more repeat and adjacent work. At the current valuation, it is too cheap vs. peers.

MWH is not just an EPC story

The first thing to note is that MWH is not just a solar EPC business. MWH does not simply build a plant and disappear afterwards. MWH benefits from the full 35-year life of a power plant, and that matters because it changes how the earnings stream should be viewed. So far, MWH has already built more than 500 power plants with a total capacity of more than 21 GW, and it is under contract to manage more than 20 GW across 150 operating plants. This tells us that this is an installed base business with a recurring revenue stream, which makes it a higher-quality business vs. a normal EPC player. Also, once a customer relies on MWH to keep the plant running well, the relationship becomes harder to replace.

The unit economics of the recurring service business are also very healthy. On the recent call, management said illustrative third-party unit economics show an average $0.82 per watt EPC build can translate into a lifecycle revenue opportunity of $1.19. By my math, that is ~45% higher revenue opportunity over the life of the asset, and they also said the current O&M portfolio alone could represent ~$7.4 billion of customer spend over a 35-year life, while only about $540 million of that is currently in backlog. This is a massive gap that suggests the reported backlog does not capture the full earnings runway. For perspective, MWH’s FY2025 revenue is $2.5 billion.

Continuing on the point of backlog, the current $8 billion backlog (as of FY2025) is enough to cover MWH’s 2026 guidance of $3.72 billion to $3.82 billion of revenue. The quality of this backlog is also extremely high, in my view, as 100% of the backlog is tied to Safe Harbor projects based on conversations with customers. For those unaware, Safe Harbor basically means those projects should be less exposed to near-term policy changes. As readers may already know, there are a lot of policy changes in the renewable energy space, so for MWH, it is great to hear that for the next couple of years, this is less of a worry.

There was another data point from the recent call that I think helps put the $8 billion in better context. Management said the backlog really represents a 24- to 30-month window because of the speed at which it can deploy projects. This suggests the figure is tied to what SOLV can actually build in its normal deployment window, which means the underlying backlog is probably way higher if we include every project customers may eventually move forward with.

Scale Should Widen the Moat From Here

I expect MWH’s moat to widen as it scales because utility-scale projects are very likely going to get bigger (to accommodate the growing electrification needs and AI data centers), while the number of contractors that can handle them is not. My view is simple. The bigger the projects get, the more customers need a contractor with real execution history, a deep pool of labor to ensure timelines are met, and, importantly, the balance sheet to support the entire project. This basically means sub-scale players will start to lose out simply because they do not have scale.

I also believe this would put MWH in a better position to win the next project (from its existing clients), and that’s not only because of its track record. As customers move toward larger and more complex projects, they are also more likely to stay with a contractor that already knows their standards, has worked with their teams, and has proven it can deliver. In essence, this lowers customer risk. You can already see this happening. Backlog growth is being driven by larger project sizes rather than a big jump in project count, which means MWH is capturing more project budget share, and it is also winning adjacent projects. You can see that from the sharp growth in T&D revenue, growing from $45 million in 2024 to $149.5 million in 2025; standalone battery storage revenue, growing from $81 million to $96 million; and more than $2.1 billion of backlog now tied to solar-plus-storage and standalone storage projects.

Valuation

It may be because MWH just IPO-ed recently, so the market is still catching on to its story, but I don’t think it should be just worth 24x forward PE. MWH is operating in the bottleneck part of the value chain, and this bottleneck (labor expertise to build utilities) is not going away anytime soon. If we look at other peers like Argan, Quanta Services, MasTec, Centuri Holdings, etc., they are all trading at 40x NTM PE and above. Over the next 24 months, MWH actually has the highest cumulative growth expectation (>60%) vs. these peers with an average of 30+%. Just comparing it this way, MWH deserves to at least trade in line at 40x NTM PE. Even if we haircut it to 30x, we can still very easily bridge to >20% upside.

Investment Risk

If the project mix shifts toward more new project starts and less service work, margins could see negative mix-shift pressure, and that would weigh on earnings growth. Another risk is on the sponsor side of things. American Securities still owns a large stake, so any future share sales or sponsor-driven decisions could weigh on the stock even if the underlying business continues to perform well.

Conclusion

I am bullish on MWH. It already has enough backlog to support near-term growth, and importantly, each project can lead to a longer-lasting customer relationship through O&M and adjacent scopes. With the stock still valued at a cheap multiple vs. peers, I think the setup remains attractive as MWH proves it can turn scale, repeat work, and services into durable earnings growth.

data.title: Morgan Stanley: A Strong Company At A Fair Price
data.description:
Shares of Morgan Stanley (MS) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining 68%. The company’s focus on wealth and asset management has helped to deliver steady results, thanks to recurring revenue streams while also providing exposure to rising market levels. At the same time, elevated volatility and increased M&A have boosted its Wall Street operations. Both strengths were on display in in a strong Q1, sending shares up over 2% in early trading. Given my view many of these strengths were priced in, I cut Morgan Stanley to a “hold” in January, and shares are up modestly since then, consistent with my rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit MS.

In the company’s first quarter, Morgan Stanley earned $3.43, which blew past estimates by $0.41 as revenue surged 16% to $20.6 billion. MS continues to generate excellent operating leverage with pretax margins up to 34% from 32% last year. Its wealth management business again reported strong organic growth with $118 million billion of inflows, including $54 billion into its fee-based strategies. Additionally, MS set aside $98 million for credit losses, down from $135 million in Q1 of last year. Unlike commercial banks, it has limited traditional lending, and its exposure to private credit is modest, insulating results from broader credit fears.

Wealth management provides steady fee-based growth

Wealth management is the core of Morgan Stanley’s business, in my view, and its strategic shift into this area has provided more recurring fee-based revenue than Wall Street peers. Revenue was up 16% from last year to $8.5 billion. Fee-based AUM is up to $2.8 trillion, up 19% from last year. Margins were a solid 30.4% with pre-tax income coming in at $2.6 billion. Thanks to higher AUM levels, asset management fees were up 16% to $5.1 billion, and transaction fees were also strong at $1.1 billion due to elevated customer trading.

MS also generates substantial economics in this segment off of custody cash and making asset-supported loans to its high-net-worth clients. Net interest income was up to $2.17 billion, 14% higher than a year ago. For several years, client cash balances have come under pressure given higher rates in money market funds, and it appears aa bottom has been reached here. Plus, efforts to grow lending options to its clients has helped to boost yields and offset the lower fed funds rate. Separately, MS continues to manage costs well. While compensation generally grows in-line with fees, non-compensation expenses were actually down $60 million to $1.27 billion, supporting the strong margin performance in Q1.

I would note that advisory fees are charged in arrears, generally based on quarter end levels. The S&P 500 closed Q1 just over 6,500, below the end of Q4. This will be a sequential headwind for Q2 earnings, as fees are largely driven off that Q1 print. We have seen the market bounce since then, and if that holds, it will lead to a rise in Q3 revenue, all else equal. Obviously though, there is a long way to go before June 30th.

Performance fees drag on investment management

Its investment management business was actually down a modest 4% to $1.54 billion. I view this unit as likely to grow structurally slower given the ongoing pressure from passive strategies, which have limited flows and contributed to longer term pricing pressure. Indeed, AUM was up a much stronger 13%, outpacing revenue substantially, speaking to the pressure on fees. Long-term flows were positive at $3.3 billion, better than many firms struggling with outflows, but this is still quite modest.

Beyond this, the unit faced a $112 million drop in performance fees to $39 million due to weaker returns in private funds. Given some of the pressure in private markets, I would not be surprised to see performance fees across the industry run at more modest levels. Now, because of this, compensation was also down $38 million to $630 million. Absent the drop in performance fees, I would have expected a ~$20 million increase. On net, lower performance fees reduced pretax income by about ~$54 million. This more than fully explains the $43 million drop to $280 million. Essentially, modest growth ex-performance fees was overwhelmed by the drop in performance fees.

Volatility and M&A activity were further tailwinds

In its Institutional Securities business, Morgan Stanley clearly benefited from the elevated volatility after the outbreak of the war with Iran. Total revenue surged 19% from last year to $10.7 billion with pre-tax income up over 25% to $4.2 billion. Fixed income revenue was up a remarkable 90% sequentially and 29% from last year to $3.36 billion—commodities and FX trading sit inside this segment. The significant volatility in oil likely contributed to results here. Equity trading was up 25% from last year to $5.15 billion. MS is benefiting from strong client trading levels, especially in options and futures. Q2 results face a tough comparison given the remarkable volatility last year following “Liberation Day,” but the trading environment remains favorable.

Investment banking revenue also surged 36% from last year to $2.1 billion. Advisory revenue was up nearly 80% to $978 million as it receives payments from deals undertaken last year that began closing. With M&A still elevated, several major deals pending, and continued dealmaking (especially in banks as well as consumer-facing companies), I expect advisory revenue to be strong all year.

Equity underwriting rose about $80 million to $396 million, and with potential blockbuster IPOs later this year like SpaceX and OpenAI, fees should remain strong. Fixed income underwriting was also solid, up about 10% to $742 million. With the tech sector generating less free cash flow than in the past due to AI cap-ex spending, debt issuance is likely to stay structurally higher for some time, a benefit to this segment. Plus, commercial real estate activity has improved, another tailwind. As such, I expect continued growth in investment banking this year.

Capital supports buybacks; shares remain a suitable hold

Morgan Stanley maintains an excellent balance sheet with a tier 1 common equity capital position of 15.1%. This is substantially above its 11.8% regulatory minimum, enabling it to return all earnings to shareholders. I also see potential regulatory relief freeing up another $5+ of capital for returns. In Q1, it did $1.75 billion of buybacks. I now expect ~$6 billion of buybacks, up from my prior $5 billion target. Thanks to repurchases, its share count is down just over 1.5% from last year. Shares also offer a 2.2% dividend yield. I view this payout as highly secure.

Back in January, I was targeting $10.60-$11.10 in 2026 earnings. This drove my $185-190 fair value target as I believe a 17x multiple is appropriate given its fee-centric revenue. This outlook assumed a normal volatility environment, and thus far, the year has been anything but normal. As a result, I expect H1 trading activity to be substantially better. Buybacks also started the year a bit more aggressively, which will keep the average share count slightly lower. Partially offsetting this, Q2 advisory fees will be negatively impacted by 3/31 market levels and performance fees may stay muted.

Given these factors, I am now targeting $11.10-$11.60 in EPS this year. I continue to view ~17x as the appropriate multiple and am raising my price target to $195. As such, I continue to see moderate upside at current levels, making shares a suitable hold, but not enough upside to rate MS a “buy.” I would use pullbacks like we saw in March to add shares as MS’s focus on traditional wealth management leaves it fairly insulated from private credit fears as well as market volatility. Morgan Stanley proved again in Q1 the strength of its model, and it remains a strong company at a fair price.

data.title: McDonald's: On Track To 50,000 Stores And Dividend King Status, But Risks Are Mounting
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered McDonald’s (MCD), I highlighted how its “Dividend King Status Nears, But Premium Valuation Meets Rising Political Risk,” rating them a Hold as their valuation didn’t seem to offer a compelling enough argument to buy yet given the macro pressure and long-term risks.

Following a solid report highlighting the company's resilience as a business despite the macro pressure, MCD’s valuation implies modest potential from current levels, with the risk-reward continuing to be unattractive while the risks stemming from the environment are rising.

On The Path to 50,000

MCD reported a solid Q4 report, beating the market’s EPS and revenue estimates, with free cash flow reaching $7.19 billion, a solid increase compared to the $6.67 billion seen in 2024 despite the significant jump in CAPEX, the latter coming as a result of the company exceeding their development plan and opening nearly 2,300 restaurants towards their target of 50,000 restaurants by the end of 2027.

This comes as MCD reported a 3.1% increase in global sales, with 2.1% comparable US sales growth, 3.2% for international operated markets (especially in Germany and Australia) and 4.6% for international developmental licensed markets, especially in Japan. The company continued its real estate investments that are ultimately key to its business model while advancing on its Accelerating the Arches strategic plan, as highlighted before, being on track to achieve the company’s fastest period of unit growth from 2024 to 2027 alongside its digital and drive thru expansion while expecting solid margins.

In a move to better compete with peers and adapt to the evolving consumer demands, McDonald’s is reportedly planning to offer new energy drinks and specialty sodas this year, which can offer a lot of potential for what are already fast-growing segments of the beverage industry while also reportedly planning to sell them at lower prices than rivals like Starbucks (SBUX) and Dutch Bros (BROS).

Although I don’t expect this to offer some major boost in sales, I still believe it highlights that their management is not doing the mistake of Nike’s (NKE) former CEO - which, as noted previously, relied on “Nike being Nike” and buybacks to deliver EPS growth, and we saw how that worked out - being willing to adapt and meet the evolving consumer needs.

Financially, based on MCD’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with current assets around their current liabilities and a lot of assets to cover their well-spread, low-interest debt.

The company also spent virtually everything they made in FCF on shareholder returns, spending $5.12 billion for the dividend and $2.06 billion for buybacks, for a total yield of roughly 3.32% based on today’s price, which is not that much, but it’s all they can do without using the balance sheet. The company remains a few quarters away from becoming a Dividend King, with their 50th consecutive annual dividend increase likely coming in late 2026, which will make them an official king.

McDonald’s next earnings report is expected on May 7, with the market estimating a Normalized EPS of $2.76 and $6.49 billion in revenue. I don’t expect major changes in their plans or numbers, but I will pay attention to how they aim to face the current environment, especially given the ongoing Iran conflict that can affect the global economy.

The Real Test: Near-Term Macro Pressure

Although MCD has proven its stability so far, the ongoing Iran conflict remains a major factor to pay attention to given its implications on the global economy and elevated level of risk it comes with, while the IMF warned recently that the world is already heading towards a worse scenario, which implies a 2.5% growth rate in global GDP this year.

This is even worse given the current consumer environment, as we’re talking about pressure that can go far beyond higher pump prices, with the inflationary shock that can come as a result potentially leading to higher rates or at the very least a pause in rate cuts, affecting borrowing costs while the Fed tries to fight the inflation that would limit the company’s consumers’ budgets.

This has already resulted in trade-downs and pressure that can push more and more people to avoid dining out or ordering fast-food and instead cook at home in an effort to save money, especially while retailers like Walmart (WMT) and most other majors were quick to capitalize on this phenomenon and offer their own cheaper or higher-value private label brands.

However, I highlighted before how McDonald’s was still holding strong in an environment where peers like Wendy’s (WEN) are downsizing in an effort to cut underperforming locations, which is far from what McDonald’s is doing. Recently, the company also announced their new under-$3 menu items and $4 breakfast deal, helping them further address the ongoing consumer trends through more value-oriented items.

Other than MAHA risks mentioned before and potential to see more pressure on the company to further adapt its portfolio to a more health-conscious audience in the future, there’s also the GLP-1 factor. A recent study from Morgan Stanley estimates that GLP-1 medication usage will reach about 15% of the US population by 2035, which may seem like a major point of concern, but I’d like to argue otherwise.

First of all, although I would note that we don’t have enough long-term data yet since we’re talking about a new risk factor, the impact on the spending for fast-food and limited-service restaurants dropped by about 8% for GLP-1 users - even though 54% of these users reported that they're dining out less or significantly less - which is far from an issue on a 15% adoption over a whole decade, being something the company can likely offset through pricing alone.

Secondly, and probably just as important, I just mentioned above two ways in which McDonald’s adapted to the current consumer environment and trends (i.e. energy drinks and value items), and I find it very hard to believe that this company out of everyone else would not adapt their portfolio to offer lower-calorie, high-protein, or whatever menu items would meet the new demand, especially since it’s quite easy for them to tweak their portfolio in that direction while still keeping their brand identity.

Risks Reiterated

Although we’re talking about what may as well be the best-in-class, McDonald’s is not immune to macro risks, as competition from several angles remains significant while the environment is tough to navigate.

Meanwhile, the consumer environment can also worsen significantly following the Iran conflict, which can lead to effects that not even McDonald’s can offset that well, while many restaurant peers are already struggling to some degree.

Valuation

For MCD’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from their $7.19 billion FCF seen in 2025, I’ll assume no changes in net borrowings given their balance sheet, with an 8.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (similar to the market's expectations), backed by their expansion plan, normalizing CAPEX, margin improvements, buybacks boosting the final per share results and potential macro improvements, followed by a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3% terminal growth rate given their international expansion and pricing power.

Given their strong leadership position but also macro pressure, let’s demand an 8.5% discount rate, with 714.2 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $182.86 billion, for an intrinsic value of $256.03 per share, which is still below the current $302.06 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

MCD remains a Hold, as the valuation seems to reflect a low potential return if everything goes according to expectations, while macro risks are piling up.

Although they are still pretty much as good as it gets when it comes to a quality business in this industry, the premium valuation makes the risk-reward level unattractive for me, while not even this company is immune to a bad enough environment.

data.title: Economic Warfare Could Lead To A Global Depression
data.description:
Economic warfare

The US-Iran war is transitioning to the next phase, and that's economic warfare. This unsuspected escalation could lead to a global depression.

Specifically, the US is now administrating a blockade of Iranian ports, with inbound and outbound traffic, and threatening other countries doing business with Iran with sanctions and secondary tariffs.

Iran is still blocking the Strait of Hormuz, which is causing a global energy supply shock.

If this economic warfare continues, a global economy will face an inflationary shock as price of crude oil spikes, and then a depression as demand collapses. Depression means a very deep and prolonged negative economic growth.

How did we get here?

The main problem is that Israel does not want a nuclear Iran - that's an existential threat to Isreal.

We need to understand what exactly the nuclear issue is with Iran.

Iran currently does not have a nuclear weapon. But there are key three terms to understand:

A threshold state

A threshold state is one that does not have a nuclear weapon, but it has all the technical capabilities to build one.

Iran is currently a threshold state: 1) enriches uranium to 60%, 2) has hundreds of kilograms of enriched uranium stockpiled, 3) operates advanced centrifuges, 4) has advanced facilities like Fordow, 5) has the scientific and industrial base, and 5) IAEA access is restricted, reducing transparency.

Breakout time

Breakout time measures the minimum time needed to produce enough weapons‑grade uranium (25 kg at 90%) for one nuclear weapon.

Current estimate Iran’s nuclear breakout time is 1–3 months under current conditions, and potentially as short as 2 weeks in a "sprint" scenario, and it was 9–12 months under JCPOA‑style constraints.

Irreversible immunity

A country has a nuclear weapon, second-strike capability, and no feasible pre-emptive strike can neutralize it. Iran does not have irreversible immunity - and this is exactly what Israel wants to prevent form happening.

Iran war Phase 1 - attempted regime change

The original mission was to decapitate Iranian leadership and force regime change, this mission failed, as regime survived despite many of its' leaders being eliminated.

Iran war Phase 2 - regional war

Iran retaliated by attacking regional energy infrastructure and the US assets in the region and closed the Strait of Hormuz to inflict economic pain to the global economy, as leverage.

Ceasefire and negotiations

The economic consequences of Strait of Hormuz closure forced a 2-week ceasefire and negotiations - to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. However, the nuclear issue prevented the deal.

The negotiations centered on extending the Iran's breakout time to 9-12 months.

Iran could possibly agree on a 5‑year moratorium: cap enrichment at ≤3.67–5%, reduce/export enriched stockpiles, freeze advanced centrifuges, restore IAEA inspections. However, Iran also asks for guarantees and sanctions relief.

However, the US wants a 20-year moratorium, with more stringent terms.

The deal is unlikely, because: 1) Iran want to preserve the threshold state status with temporary delay 2) Isreal want to take way the threshold state status completely, 3) while the US could accept a very long freeze, which would be practically permanent.

The next phase

Currently, the US and Iran are planning for a second round of negotiations before the ceasefire expires next week. These negotiations are strictly to extend the ceasefire, as the nuclear deal is unreachable.

Given that Iran has a very short-term breakout time, the situation is urgent to act.

One option for the US is to take the enriched uranium by force, and to open Hormuz with force.

Second option is to force the Iranian regime to accept the terms of negotiations by essentially starving the country and causing internal uprising - this would threaten the Iranian regime.

How do you do this? Naval blockade to prevent Iran's oil exports, and also to block Iran's imports. Also, by sanctioning all countries that try to help Iran in any way. That's economic warfare, and it's currently the preferred option.

Will it work?

Economic warfare could be an effective strategy to force Iran to the negotiating table and to accept the nuclear proposals. However, it could take a long time until Iran is ready for negotiations under duress of internal economic crisis.

During this period, Iran is likely to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed as well. Thus, the energy supply shock will worsen, in addition to the shortages of fertilizers, helium, and other key products.

Thus, the question is whether the global economy will be able to tolerate this situation.

Essentially, the US and Iran have engaged in a game of whose economy will "crack" first. Will Iran "crack" first under the blockade or will the US "crack" first under the inflationary shock.

Obviously, this game is leading to a disaster economically. The only hope for a deal is that Iran agrees to extend offered 5-year moratorium to 10 years, and US provides more guarantees, but this is still very unlikely.

Implications

So, we have the S&P500 (SP500) at the all-time highs, above the pre-war levels, while oil is 50% higher. Also note, that physical crude oil trading near $150/barrel, so even oil futures at $90 are trading based on hope.

The stock market rallied after the ceasefire was announced, rallied again after the talk were announced, rallied again after the signals for second round of talks (after the first round failed), and rallied again on the same hope.

The hope is that the deal will be made, Hormuz will reopen, oil prices will fall back to 50s - all possibly next week or within a month.

The reality is that the deal will be very hard to make, but the ceasefire is likely to be extended to allow the economic warfare strategy to work.

Economic warfare means inflation will likely accelerate as oil continues to rise and stays high - as long as Iran as able to survive the blockade, and this could be a long time.

Ultimately, this game will lead to demand collapse as countries are forced to ration energy, which means a deep prolong recession or a depression.

What's the alternative? Taking the Hormuz by force and military escalation. This is even worse scenario, as it would push oil prices much higher, and cause even higher inflation.

The US cannot declare victory and just leave - with Iran's breakout time measured in weeks.

I really don't see any other positive outcomes. The only hope is that Iran silently abandons its nuclear aspirations and gets financially rewarded - at the next round of negotiations. Later is too late, once inflation persistently spikes.

The market sees this scenario this as a baseline - and if it's wrong, we could see a sharp pullback by 10% before a deep bear market takes over.
